summary: Graham argues that procrastination thrives on distractions that evolve over time, especially as the Internet has become an ever-present, highly addictive time sink. Because distractions actively seek you out and rules tend to fail, the key is to make the habit visible and harder to slip into. He proposes separating internet use onto a different computer so the main work machine remains distraction-free.
key_points: Distractions are dynamic and get more compelling as technology improves, so old defenses stop working.
The Internet feels like work, making its distractions harder to notice and easier to rationalize.
Rules and willpower fail because addictive behaviors exploit any available path.
Visibility and friction are essential to breaking habits; you must notice each slip.
Physically separating internet use from work creates enough awareness to reduce time online.
money_quotes: Distraction is not a static obstacle that you avoid like you might avoid a rock in the road.

Addictive things have to be treated as if they were sentient adversaries—as if there were a little man in your head always cooking up the most plausible arguments for doing whatever you're trying to stop doing.
The key seems to be visibility.
When I have to sit on the other side of the room to check email or browse the web, I become much more aware of it.
key_insight: To curb procrastination in a distraction-rich world, you need structural friction and visibility, not just willpower.
